content.text: Stickiness vs Adoption — Why They Are Different Problems
The adoption funnel tells you how many people have reached each stage. Stickiness tells you
whether they stay there

. A tool can have excellent activation (many people try it) and terrible stickiness (almost none return). These require completely different interventions — you cannot fix a stickiness problem with better onboarding, and you cannot fix an activation problem by making the tool more habit-forming.

Stickiness is the ratio of daily active users to monthly active users (DAU/MAU). A stickiness of 50% means that on any given day, half of that month's active users are using the tool. It is the single most informative metric for whether GenAI has become part of daily work.

This document covers how to compute DAU/MAU and related engagement metrics, how to segment by cohort to distinguish structural patterns from noise, and how to identify the specific engagement patterns that predict long-term embedding versus slow churn.
Stickiness Curves by Cohort

attrs.alt: Stickiness Curves: DAU/MAU by Cohort Over 6 Months

content.text: Four BU cohorts show four different stickiness trajectories. Technology stabilises at 65% — daily-habit territory. Finance shows a novelty spike in month one, then a steep decline that stabilises at 31% — the classic enthusiasm-then-attrition pattern. Legal starts low and climbs slowly. Operations is steady in the healthy range.

Each trajectory implies a different intervention. Finance needs habit-building, not more promotion. Legal needs use case discovery. Operations needs maintenance, not active intervention. Technology is a candidate for best-practice extraction and case studies.
The Core Stickiness Metrics
DAU/MAU Ratio
DAU/MAU is computed as a rolling ratio, not a period-end snapshot. Use a 30-day rolling window for MAU and a 1-day window for DAU, then average the daily DAU/MAU ratio over the reporting month.
WAU/MAU Ratio

For tools used less than daily by nature (e.g. a document-generation tool used for formal reports), DAU/MAU will be low even for embedded users. Use WAU/MAU (weekly active users / monthly active users) as the primary stickiness metric for these tool types. A tool used twice per week by embedded users has a WAU/MAU of ~50%, which is healthy, even if its DAU/MAU is only 15%.
Tool Type
Natural Usage Frequency
Primary Stickiness Metric
Healthy Range
Email / chat assistant
Multiple times daily
DAU/MAU
Document drafting
Several times per week
WAU/MAU
Code generation
Daily for developers
DAU/MAU
Report generation
Weekly or monthly
MAU retention
80%+ monthly return rate
Use case ideation / research
Ad-hoc, project-based
Session depth (time/session)
N/A for DAU/MAU
Cohort Analysis — Tracking Engagement Over Time

A cohort is a group of users who started using a tool in the same calendar month. Cohort analysis reveals whether later cohorts (benefiting from improved onboarding, better champions, more use case examples) have better stickiness than earlier ones.

The key question cohort analysis answers: is the programme getting better over time? If month-3 retention is consistently improving across successive cohorts, your interventions (better onboarding, more champions, richer artefact library) are working. If it is flat or declining, the problem is structural, not operational.
Engagement Depth Metrics
Stickiness (DAU/MAU) measures frequency. Engagement depth measures
quality

of each session. High-frequency, low-depth usage (many quick queries, all of the same trivial type) is very different from high-frequency, high-depth usage (multi-turn conversations, complex prompts, output edited and used in downstream work).
Depth Metric
Formula
What It Reveals
Session duration (median)
Median time from first interaction to last interaction per session
Whether users are engaging in multi-step tasks or one-shot queries
Turns per session (median)
Median number of prompt-response pairs per session
Whether users are iterating on outputs or accepting first-draft responses
Output utilisation rate
Proportion of sessions where the output was copied/exported vs sessions where no export action followed
Whether the tool is producing outputs that enter the user's real workflow
Prompt length trend
Average prompt character count over time for a cohort
Whether users are learning to write better, more detailed prompts (a proxy for skill growth)

Output utilisation rate is the most actionable depth metric. If fewer than 40% of sessions result in the output being copied, exported, or used in a downstream document, the tool is being explored rather than used. The intervention is use case sharpening: show users specific tasks where the output directly enters a deliverable.
Identifying At-Risk Users Before They Churn
The most valuable stickiness intervention is
pre-churn detection
: identifying users whose engagement is declining before they stop entirely, and reaching out before they disengage. The signal is a falling usage frequency over a rolling window.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class ChurnRiskDetector:
    user_id: str
    # Active days counts for recent windows
    active_days_last_7: int
    active_days_prior_7: int    # days 8-14 before today
    active_days_last_30: int
    active_days_prior_30: int   # days 31-60 before today

    def weekly_decline(self) -> float:
        """Negative = declining usage."""
        return self.active_days_last_7 - self.active_days_prior_7

    def monthly_decline(self) -> float:
        return self.active_days_last_30 - self.active_days_prior_30

    def churn_risk(self) -> str:
        if self.active_days_last_30 == 0:
            return "CHURNED"
        if self.weekly_decline() < -2 and self.monthly_decline() < -5:
            return "HIGH_RISK"
        if self.monthly_decline() < -3:
            return "MEDIUM_RISK"
        if self.active_days_last_7 == 0 and self.active_days_last_30 > 0:
            return "LAPSING"  # was active, gone quiet this week
        return "HEALTHY"

    def recommended_action(self) -> str:
        risk = self.churn_risk()
        actions = {
            "CHURNED":     "Trigger re-engagement survey: what stopped you using the tool?",
            "HIGH_RISK":   "Champion outreach: offer a personalised use case conversation this week",
            "MEDIUM_RISK": "Send prompt-of-the-week email; invite to next office hours",
            "LAPSING":     "Low-touch nudge: share a new artefact relevant to their role",
            "HEALTHY":     "No action needed",
        }
        return actions[risk]

# Example
user = ChurnRiskDetector(
    user_id="U-4821",
    active_days_last_7=1,
    active_days_prior_7=4,
    active_days_last_30=12,
    active_days_prior_30=18,
)
print(user.churn_risk())           # HIGH_RISK
print(user.recommended_action())   # Champion outreach: offer a personalised use case conversation this week
Common Stickiness Anti-Patterns
The Novelty Spike

Almost every GenAI tool deployment shows a DAU/MAU spike in month one — curiosity drives first sessions. The spike then collapses as users who found no compelling daily use case disengage. A month-one spike followed by a sharp month-two decline is not a stickiness problem; it is an activation-quality problem (too many users activated for curiosity rather than genuine utility). Fix it by improving the quality of first sessions, not by running re-engagement campaigns.
The Plateau Below Target

A cohort that stabilises at 20% DAU/MAU (where the target is 40-60%) is not declining — it has found its equilibrium for that population. The users still active are using the tool on the right days for them. The intervention is not to push the 20% users to use it more; it is to ask why the other 80% stopped and whether there is a use case that would make the tool relevant to them. If not, the tool may genuinely not fit that role's workflow, and the honest answer is to stop measuring it as a failure and focus on roles where it does.
The Hollow MAU

A high MAU with very low DAU/MAU (e.g. MAU = 400 users, DAU/MAU = 8%) means a large pool of users who open the tool once a month or less. These users are technically 'monthly active' but are not meaningfully engaged. Track the distribution of sessions per user per month, not just the count of users with at least one session. A healthy distribution has most users in the 8-20 sessions/month range; a hollow MAU has most users at 1-2 sessions with a long tail.
Building a Stickiness Dashboard

Collecting the metrics is the easy part — most modern platform telemetry systems expose the raw events needed. The hard part is synthesising them into a dashboard that programme leads can act on without wading through raw data. A well-designed stickiness dashboard has three layers:
Executive layer (one screen): aggregate DAU/MAU trend line for the full organisation, colour-coded against target band (40-60%), with a one-line intervention if below band.
BU layer (drill-down): DAU/MAU by BU for the last three months, with cohort comparison showing whether the most recent cohort has better M3 retention than the prior one.
User-level layer (access-controlled): at-risk user list generated by the churn risk detector, visible only to programme team and assigned champions — never to managers or HR.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class StickinessDashboard:
    as_of: date
    overall_dau_mau: float
    target_dau_mau_low: float = 0.40
    target_dau_mau_high: float = 0.60
    bu_stickiness: dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)
    # Cohort M3 retention comparison
    cohort_m3_retention: dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict)
    # At-risk user count (not identities — for executive layer)
    at_risk_count: int = 0
    churned_count: int = 0

    def traffic_light(self) -> str:
        if self.overall_dau_mau >= self.target_dau_mau_low:
            return "GREEN"
        if self.overall_dau_mau >= self.target_dau_mau_low * 0.75:
            return "AMBER"
        return "RED"

    def executive_summary(self) -> str:
        status = self.traffic_light()
        pct = self.overall_dau_mau * 100
        lines = [
            f"Overall stickiness: {pct:.0f}% DAU/MAU  [{status}]",
            f"Target band: {self.target_dau_mau_low*100:.0f}-{self.target_dau_mau_high*100:.0f}%",
        ]
        if status == "RED":
            lines.append("Intervention: Champion outreach to at-risk users; run 'what got in the way?' survey this week")
        elif status == "AMBER":
            lines.append("Intervention: Monitor weekly; champion check-ins with lapsing users")
        else:
            lines.append("Status: On track — maintain cadence")
        if self.at_risk_count:
            lines.append(f"At-risk users (flagged for champion outreach): {self.at_risk_count}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

    def cohort_improvement(self) -> str:
        if len(self.cohort_m3_retention) < 2:
            return "Insufficient cohort data"
        months = sorted(self.cohort_m3_retention.keys())
        first, last = months[0], months[-1]
        delta = self.cohort_m3_retention[last] - self.cohort_m3_retention[first]
        direction = "IMPROVING" if delta > 0.02 else ("DECLINING" if delta < -0.02 else "STABLE")
        return (
            f"M3 retention {direction}: "
            f"{first}={self.cohort_m3_retention[first]:.0%}, "
            f"{last}={self.cohort_m3_retention[last]:.0%} "
            f"(delta: {delta:+.0%})"
        )

dash = StickinessDashboard(
    as_of=date(2025, 6, 30),
    overall_dau_mau=0.38,
    bu_stickiness={"Technology": 0.65, "Operations": 0.34, "Finance": 0.31, "Legal": 0.26},
    cohort_m3_retention={"2025-01": 0.38, "2025-02": 0.42, "2025-03": 0.47},
    at_risk_count=34,
    churned_count=12,
)
print(dash.executive_summary())
print(dash.cohort_improvement())
# Overall stickiness: 38% DAU/MAU  [AMBER]
# Target band: 40-60%
# Intervention: Monitor weekly; champion check-ins with lapsing users
# At-risk users (flagged for champion outreach): 34
# M3 retention IMPROVING: 2025-01=38%, 2025-03=47% (delta: +9%)

The cohort improvement indicator (M3 retention IMPROVING) is the most important signal in this example. Even though the overall stickiness is AMBER, the programme is getting better at producing retained users — the early cohorts had poor retention, but the interventions since then have raised M3 retention by 9 percentage points. This is the narrative that matters for a steering committee: the trend is right even if the absolute number is not yet at target.
Stickiness vs Quality — Avoiding the Engagement Trap

High stickiness is not always good. A tool with a DAU/MAU of 70% that users are opening compulsively to check for errors in its outputs, or to re-run prompts because the first result was poor, is generating high engagement for the wrong reason. Pair every stickiness metric with at least one quality signal:
Net Promoter Score (NPS) from monthly pulse: if NPS is negative while stickiness is high, users are trapped, not delighted.
Rework rate: how often do users edit or discard AI outputs before using them? Rising rework rate with rising stickiness is a quality signal, not a success.
Escalation rate: if users increasingly seek champion or IT support to fix tool outputs, high session counts may reflect frustration loops rather than productive use.
Output utilisation rate (from the depth metrics section): if utilisation is falling while sessions are rising, users are browsing the tool without producing usable outputs.

The goal is not maximum stickiness — it is stickiness at the right level for the tool's natural use frequency, with quality metrics confirming that the engagement is productive. A document drafting tool with a WAU/MAU of 50% and a 75% output utilisation rate is a success. The same tool with WAU/MAU of 50% and a 25% output utilisation rate is a problem.
Summary

Stickiness tracks whether users who adopted a GenAI tool keep using it at a frequency that suggests genuine integration into daily work. The primary metric is DAU/MAU (or WAU/MAU for naturally less-frequent tools), segmented by cohort and BU to reveal structural patterns rather than aggregate noise. Cohort analysis answers whether successive adoption waves have improved retention — a rising M3 retention trend is evidence that programme interventions are working.

Engagement depth (session duration, turns per session, output utilisation rate, prompt length trend) distinguishes meaningful usage from tool exploration. Pre-churn detection — identifying users with declining weekly engagement before they stop entirely — is the most cost-effective stickiness intervention: a champion conversation at MEDIUM_RISK costs far less than a re-engagement campaign at CHURNED.
